name: Diabetes Mellitus Type 1
signs:
Polyuria and polydipsia
Weight loss despite increased appetite
Fatigue
Blurred vision
DKA at presentation (nausea, vomiting, abdominal pain, Kussmaul breathing)
Young age at onset
No obesity (typically)
treatment:
1. Insulin therapy (mandatory, lifelong)
2. Basal-bolus regimen or insulin pump
3. Continuous glucose monitoring (CGM)
4. Carbohydrate counting
5. HbA1c target <7%
6. Screen for complications annually
7. Screen for autoimmune comorbidities (thyroid, celiac)
meds:
Insulin glargine 0.2-0.4 units/kg SC daily (basal)
Insulin lispro/aspart dosed per carb ratio (bolus, ~1:10-1:15)
Insulin pump therapy (continuous SC infusion)
Glucagon 1mg IM/SC (severe hypoglycemia)
Automated insulin delivery (hybrid closed-loop systems)
ddx: Type 2 diabetes, LADA, MODY, Secondary diabetes (pancreatitis, CF), DKA from other causes
labs:
Random glucose >=200 + symptoms, FPG >=126, or HbA1c >=6.5%
C-peptide (low/absent)
GAD65, IA-2, ZnT8, IAA antibodies (autoimmune markers)
HbA1c q3 months
Annual: urine albumin, lipids, TSH, celiac serologies
Comprehensive metabolic panel
notes:
Autoimmune destruction of pancreatic beta cells
Autoantibodies positive in 90%+ at diagnosis
Honeymoon period: partial remission after starting insulin (months to 1-2 years)
DKA prevention: never stop basal insulin, sick day rules
Hypoglycemia unawareness: reduced counterregulatory response with recurrent lows
Type 1 screening: now possible (teplizumab can delay onset in stage 2)
Associated autoimmune: thyroiditis (25%), celiac (5-10%), Addison's, vitiligo
description: An autoimmune disease where the immune system destroys insulin-producing cells in the pancreas, requiring lifelong insulin therapy to manage blood sugar levels.

name: Addison's Disease
signs:
Fatigue and weakness
Hyperpigmentation (skin creases, gums, scars)
Weight loss and anorexia
Hypotension and orthostasis
Salt craving
Nausea, vomiting, abdominal pain
Hyponatremia and hyperkalemia
Adrenal crisis: shock, altered consciousness
treatment:
1. Glucocorticoid replacement (hydrocortisone)
2. Mineralocorticoid replacement (fludrocortisone)
3. Stress dosing during illness/surgery
4. Medical alert bracelet
5. Emergency injectable hydrocortisone for adrenal crisis
6. Patient education on sick day rules
meds:
Hydrocortisone 15-25mg daily (divided: 2/3 AM, 1/3 afternoon)
Fludrocortisone 0.05-0.2mg daily
Stress dosing: hydrocortisone 50-100mg IV/IM q8h
DHEA 25-50mg daily (women, debated)
Emergency kit: hydrocortisone 100mg IM
ddx: Secondary adrenal insufficiency (pituitary), Chronic fatigue syndrome, Depression, Hypothyroidism, Anorexia nervosa, Hemochromatosis
labs:
Morning cortisol (low <3mcg/dL diagnostic, >18 rules out)
ACTH stimulation test (gold standard: cortisol <18 at 30/60min)
ACTH level (elevated in primary, low in secondary)
BMP (hyponatremia, hyperkalemia, hypoglycemia)
21-hydroxylase antibodies (autoimmune)
Adrenal CT (hemorrhage, infiltration, calcification)
Renin and aldosterone (elevated renin, low aldo)
TSH, CBC
notes:
Primary (Addison's): autoimmune most common in developed countries (70-90%), also TB, adrenal hemorrhage
Secondary: pituitary/hypothalamic disease, chronic steroid use then abrupt withdrawal
Adrenal crisis: life-threatening - give hydrocortisone 100mg IV immediately, do not wait for labs
Autoimmune polyglandular syndrome: Addison's + thyroid disease + type 1 DM
Hyperpigmentation: ACTH cross-reacts with melanocortin receptors (absent in secondary)
Sick day rules: double or triple oral dose during illness, IM injection if vomiting
Waterhouse-Friderichsen: bilateral adrenal hemorrhage from meningococcemia
description: A rare condition where the adrenal glands don't produce enough cortisol and aldosterone, causing fatigue, low blood pressure, and darkening of the skin, with risk of life-threatening adrenal crisis.

name: Hyperparathyroidism
signs:
Often asymptomatic (found incidentally on labs)
Bones: bone pain, osteoporosis, fractures
Stones: renal calculi
Abdominal groans: nausea, constipation, pancreatitis
Psychiatric moans: depression, confusion, fatigue
Polyuria and polydipsia
treatment:
1. Surgery (parathyroidectomy) for symptomatic or meeting criteria
2. Monitoring for asymptomatic patients not meeting surgical criteria
3. Hydration
4. Calcimimetics for non-surgical candidates
5. Bisphosphonates for osteoporosis
meds:
Cinacalcet 30-90mg BID (calcimimetic, non-surgical candidates)
Alendronate 70mg weekly (osteoporosis)
IV normal saline (hypercalcemic crisis)
Furosemide 20-40mg IV (after adequate hydration)
Calcitonin 4 IU/kg SC q12h (acute hypercalcemia)
ddx: Malignancy-associated hypercalcemia (PTHrP), Vitamin D excess, Granulomatous disease, Thiazide-induced, Familial hypocalciuric hypercalcemia, Milk-alkali syndrome
labs:
Serum calcium elevated
PTH elevated (inappropriately normal = also diagnostic)
24h urine calcium (distinguish from FHH)
Vitamin D level
Phosphorus (low)
Alkaline phosphatase
BMP (creatinine)
DEXA scan
Renal ultrasound (nephrolithiasis)
Sestamibi scan (localize adenoma pre-surgery)
4D CT parathyroid (alternative localization)
notes:
Bones, Stones, Abdominal groans, Psychiatric moans (classic mnemonic)
Primary: single adenoma (85%), hyperplasia (15%), carcinoma (<1%)
Surgical indications: symptomatic, Ca >1mg/dL above normal, age <50, osteoporosis, eGFR <60, renal stones, 24h urine Ca >400
FHH: benign, autosomal dominant, low urine calcium (Ca/Cr clearance ratio <0.01) - do NOT operate
Normocalcemic PHPT: elevated PTH with normal calcium - emerging entity
Secondary hyperparathyroidism: CKD-related, PTH elevated with low/normal calcium
Tertiary: autonomous PTH after long-standing secondary (post-transplant)
description: A condition where one or more parathyroid glands produce too much parathyroid hormone, causing elevated calcium levels that can affect bones, kidneys, and mental function.

name: Diabetes Insipidus
signs:
Polyuria (3-20 L/day of dilute urine)
Polydipsia (intense thirst)
Nocturia
Dehydration if unable to access water
Hypernatremia
Preference for ice-cold water
Dilute urine despite dehydration
treatment:
1. Central DI: desmopressin (DDAVP)
2. Nephrogenic DI: treat underlying cause, low-solute diet, thiazides
3. Ensure adequate fluid access
4. Monitor sodium and urine output
5. Address underlying cause
meds:
Desmopressin (DDAVP) 10-40mcg intranasal or 0.1-0.4mg PO BID-TID (central DI)
Hydrochlorothiazide 25mg daily (nephrogenic DI - paradoxical antidiuresis)
Amiloride 5-10mg BID (lithium-induced nephrogenic DI)
Indomethacin 25mg TID (adjunct for nephrogenic DI)
Free water replacement
ddx: Primary polydipsia (psychogenic), Diabetes mellitus, Hypercalcemia, Hypokalemia, CKD, Beer potomania
labs:
Serum sodium (elevated or high-normal)
Serum osmolality (>295 mOsm/kg)
Urine osmolality (inappropriately dilute <300 in face of high serum osm)
Urine specific gravity (<1.005)
Water deprivation test (gold standard)
Desmopressin challenge (distinguishes central vs nephrogenic)
Copeptin levels (emerging test)
Brain MRI (pituitary/hypothalamic lesion)
BMP, calcium, potassium
notes:
Central: lack of ADH production (pituitary surgery, tumor, infiltrative, trauma, idiopathic)
Nephrogenic: kidney resistant to ADH (lithium most common drug cause, hypercalcemia, hypokalemia, genetic)
Water deprivation test: if urine concentrates with desmopressin = central; if not = nephrogenic
Post-surgical DI: often transient after pituitary surgery (triphasic response)
Bright spot on MRI: posterior pituitary, absent in central DI
Desmopressin: risk of hyponatremia if overcorrected - allow breakthrough polyuria
Gestational DI: placental vasopressinase degrades ADH
description: A condition characterized by excessive urination and intense thirst caused by either insufficient production of antidiuretic hormone (central) or the kidneys' inability to respond to it (nephrogenic).

name: Metabolic Syndrome
signs:
Central obesity (waist >40in men, >35in women)
Elevated blood pressure (>=130/85)
Elevated fasting glucose (>=100)
Elevated triglycerides (>=150)
Low HDL (<40 men, <50 women)
Acanthosis nigricans
Skin tags
treatment:
1. Lifestyle modification (cornerstone): diet, exercise, weight loss
2. Target 7-10% weight loss
3. Mediterranean or DASH diet
4. 150 min/week moderate-intensity exercise
5. Treat individual components as needed
6. GLP-1 RA or bariatric surgery for obesity
7. Cardiovascular risk assessment
meds:
Metformin 500-1000mg BID (if prediabetes/diabetes)
Atorvastatin 10-40mg daily (dyslipidemia)
Lisinopril 10-40mg daily (hypertension)
Semaglutide 2.4mg SC weekly (weight management)
Fenofibrate 145mg daily (hypertriglyceridemia if >500)
Icosapent ethyl 2g BID (elevated TG + CVD risk)
ddx: Cushing syndrome, Hypothyroidism, PCOS, Lipodystrophy, Medication-induced (antipsychotics, steroids)
labs:
Fasting glucose and HbA1c
Fasting lipid panel
Blood pressure measurement
Waist circumference
BMP
LFTs (NAFLD screening)
TSH
Uric acid
notes:
Defined by >=3 of 5 criteria (IDF/NHLBI harmonized definition)
Affects ~35% of US adults
Doubles cardiovascular disease risk, 5x diabetes risk
NAFLD/NASH: hepatic manifestation of metabolic syndrome
Insulin resistance is the underlying pathophysiology
Visceral adiposity more metabolically harmful than subcutaneous
Bariatric surgery: most effective intervention for severe obesity with metabolic syndrome
GLP-1 RA: cardiovascular benefit beyond weight loss (SELECT trial with semaglutide)
description: A cluster of interconnected metabolic abnormalities including central obesity, high blood pressure, high blood sugar, and abnormal cholesterol levels that together significantly increase the risk of heart disease, stroke, and diabetes.

name: Polycystic Kidney Disease
signs:
Bilateral flank masses
Hypertension (often presenting sign)
Flank or abdominal pain
Gross hematuria
Recurrent UTIs
Renal calculi
Hepatic cysts
Intracranial aneurysms
Mitral valve prolapse
treatment:
1. Blood pressure control (ACEi/ARB preferred, target <110/75 if eGFR >60)
2. Tolvaptan (slows cyst growth and eGFR decline)
3. Adequate hydration (>3L/day, suppress vasopressin)
4. Low sodium diet
5. Avoid caffeine (may stimulate cyst growth)
6. Pain management (avoid NSAIDs)
7. Renal replacement therapy/transplant for ESRD
meds:
Lisinopril 10-40mg daily (BP control)
Tolvaptan 45-120mg daily (V2 receptor antagonist, slows progression)
Acetaminophen for pain (avoid NSAIDs)
Antibiotics for cyst infections (fluoroquinolones penetrate cysts)
Statins as needed
ddx: Simple renal cysts, Autosomal recessive PKD, Tuberous sclerosis, Von Hippel-Lindau, Acquired cystic disease (dialysis), Medullary sponge kidney
labs:
Renal ultrasound (bilateral enlarged kidneys with multiple cysts)
Revised Ravine criteria (age-dependent cyst numbers)
CT or MRI (total kidney volume - htTKV for prognosis)
BMP (creatinine, eGFR monitoring)
Urinalysis
Genetic testing (PKD1, PKD2)
MRA brain (screen for intracranial aneurysm if family history)
LFTs
Lipid panel
notes:
ADPKD: PKD1 (85%, worse prognosis, ESRD ~54yo) vs PKD2 (15%, milder, ESRD ~74yo)
Most common inherited kidney disease (1/400-1/1000)
Tolvaptan (TEMPO trial): slows GFR decline 1mL/min/year, monitor LFTs (hepatotoxicity)
Intracranial aneurysm: screen with MRA if family history of SAH/aneurysm, rupture risk 5x general population
Cyst infection: fever + flank pain, CT/PET may localize, fluoroquinolones preferred (cyst penetration)
Total kidney volume (htTKV) >600mL by MRI predicts rapid progression
Native nephrectomy may be needed before transplant (massive kidneys)
description: A genetic disorder causing numerous fluid-filled cysts to develop in the kidneys, progressively enlarging them and eventually leading to kidney failure, most commonly inherited in an autosomal dominant pattern.

name: Hemolytic Uremic Syndrome
signs:
Triad: microangiopathic hemolytic anemia + thrombocytopenia + acute kidney injury
Bloody diarrhea (prodrome in typical HUS)
Oliguria or anuria
Pallor and jaundice
Petechiae
Hypertension
Seizures (severe)
Edema
treatment:
1. Supportive care is mainstay for typical (STEC-HUS)
2. IV fluids and electrolyte management
3. Dialysis for severe AKI
4. Transfuse PRBCs if symptomatic anemia (Hgb <6-7)
5. Avoid antibiotics for STEC infection (may worsen HUS)
6. Avoid platelet transfusion unless active bleeding
7. Atypical HUS: eculizumab (complement inhibitor)
meds:
IV normal saline (volume expansion early)
PRBC transfusion (Hgb <6-7)
Eculizumab 900mg IV weekly x4 then 1200mg q2wk (atypical HUS)
Ravulizumab (longer-acting complement inhibitor)
Antihypertensives (amlodipine, labetalol)
Dialysis support
Avoid: antibiotics for STEC, anti-motility agents, platelet transfusions
ddx: TTP (more neurologic, ADAMTS13 <10%), DIC, Malignant hypertension, SLE nephritis, Preeclampsia/HELLP, ITP, Scleroderma renal crisis
labs:
CBC (anemia, thrombocytopenia)
Peripheral smear (schistocytes, helmet cells)
BMP (elevated creatinine, electrolyte abnormalities)
LDH elevated
Haptoglobin decreased
Direct Coombs negative
Reticulocyte count elevated
Stool culture and Shiga toxin assay
ADAMTS13 activity (>10% = not TTP)
Complement studies (C3, C4, factor H, factor I, MCP) for atypical
Urinalysis (hematuria, proteinuria)
notes:
Typical (STEC-HUS): 90% of pediatric HUS, E. coli O157:H7 (Shiga toxin), self-limited in most
Atypical HUS: complement-mediated, no diarrheal prodrome, recurrent, treat with complement inhibitor
Antibiotic use in STEC may increase HUS risk by increasing toxin release
STEC-HUS: supportive care, 90% recover renal function
Atypical HUS: without complement inhibition, 50% progress to ESRD
Differentiate from TTP by ADAMTS13 activity: <10% = TTP, >10% = HUS
Meningococcal vaccination required before eculizumab (blocks terminal complement)
description: A condition where damaged red blood cells clog the kidneys' tiny blood vessels, causing kidney failure, most commonly triggered in children by certain strains of E. coli bacteria.

name: Spinal Stenosis
signs:
Neurogenic claudication (leg pain/heaviness with walking)
Improves with sitting or leaning forward (shopping cart sign)
Bilateral leg symptoms (often asymmetric)
Low back pain
Numbness and tingling in legs
Weakness (advanced)
Normal pulses (distinguishes from vascular claudication)
treatment:
1. Conservative first: PT, NSAIDs, activity modification
2. Epidural steroid injections for temporary relief
3. Gabapentin or pregabalin for neuropathic pain
4. Surgical decompression (laminectomy) for refractory cases
5. Fusion if concurrent instability/spondylolisthesis
6. Weight management and core strengthening
meds:
NSAIDs (naproxen 500mg BID)
Gabapentin 300-1200mg TID
Epidural steroid injection (fluoroscopic-guided)
Acetaminophen 1g QID
Duloxetine 60mg daily (neuropathic pain)
Calcitonin nasal spray 200IU daily (limited evidence)
ddx: Peripheral arterial disease (vascular claudication), Lumbar disc herniation, Peripheral neuropathy, Hip osteoarthritis, Cauda equina syndrome, Trochanteric bursitis
labs:
MRI lumbar spine (gold standard - AP canal diameter, thecal sac area)
CT myelography (if MRI contraindicated)
XR lumbar spine (spondylolisthesis, alignment)
EMG/NCV (differentiate from neuropathy)
ABI (rule out PAD if vascular claudication suspected)
notes:
Most common reason for spine surgery in patients >65
Neurogenic vs vascular claudication: neurogenic improves with flexion/sitting, vascular improves with standing still
Shopping cart sign: patients lean forward on cart (flexion opens spinal canal)
Degenerative: facet hypertrophy, ligamentum flavum thickening, disc bulging
SPORT trial: surgery superior to conservative at 4 years for symptoms, but high crossover rate
Spondylolisthesis (vertebral slippage): may require fusion in addition to decompression
Cauda equina syndrome: emergency - bowel/bladder dysfunction requires urgent decompression
description: Narrowing of the spinal canal in the lower back that compresses the nerves, causing leg pain and weakness when walking that is relieved by sitting or bending forward.

name: Cervical Radiculopathy
signs:
Neck pain radiating to arm
Dermatomal numbness/tingling
Weakness in specific myotome
Decreased deep tendon reflexes
Spurling test positive (head extension + rotation reproduces symptoms)
Relief with arm abduction (shoulder abduction sign)
Neck stiffness
treatment:
1. Conservative management first (80-90% improve)
2. NSAIDs and activity modification
3. Cervical collar for comfort (short-term only)
4. Physical therapy (traction, strengthening)
5. Cervical epidural steroid injection
6. Surgery (ACDF or posterior foraminotomy) for refractory or progressive deficit
meds:
NSAIDs (naproxen 500mg BID, meloxicam 15mg daily)
Gabapentin 300-1200mg TID (neuropathic pain)
Pregabalin 75-150mg BID
Methylprednisolone dose pack (acute, short course)
Cyclobenzaprine 10mg TID (muscle spasm)
Epidural steroid injection (interlaminar or transforaminal)
ddx: Cervical myelopathy, Thoracic outlet syndrome, Rotator cuff pathology, Carpal tunnel syndrome, Peripheral neuropathy, Brachial plexopathy, Pancoast tumor
labs:
MRI cervical spine (disc herniation, foraminal stenosis)
CT (bony detail if surgery planned)
XR cervical spine (alignment, disc space narrowing)
EMG/NCV (if diagnosis uncertain, >6 weeks)
notes:
C6 root (C5-C6 disc): biceps weakness, decreased biceps reflex, thumb/index numbness
C7 root (C6-C7 disc): most common, triceps weakness, decreased triceps reflex, middle finger numbness
C8 root (C7-T1 disc): grip weakness, finger flexor weakness, ring/pinky numbness
Spurling test: most specific physical exam maneuver
ACDF: anterior cervical discectomy and fusion - most common surgery
Myelopathy signs (cord compression): gait ataxia, hand clumsiness, Hoffmann sign, hyperreflexia → consider surgery
Red flags: progressive weakness, myelopathy, bowel/bladder symptoms → urgent MRI
description: Compression of a nerve root in the neck causing pain, numbness, and weakness radiating down the arm, most commonly from a herniated disc or bone spur.

name: Snakebite Envenomation
signs:
Fang marks
Local pain, swelling, and ecchymosis (progressive)
Bleeding from bite site
Coagulopathy (bleeding from gums, IV sites)
Nausea/vomiting
Metallic taste
Fasciculations (elapid/neurotoxic)
Ptosis and respiratory failure (elapid)
Compartment syndrome (rare)
treatment:
1. Immobilize affected extremity below heart level
2. Remove jewelry/constricting items
3. Antivenom (CroFab or Anavip for pit vipers in US)
4. Serial labs q6h (coagulopathy may recur)
5. Tetanus prophylaxis
6. Do NOT: tourniquet, incision, suction, ice
7. Observe minimum 8-12h even if initially dry bite
meds:
CroFab 4-6 vials IV initial, repeat q1h until control
Anavip 10 vials IV initial (longer half-life)
Maintenance: CroFab 2 vials at 6, 12, 18h
Morphine 2-4mg IV PRN (pain)
Tetanus prophylaxis
Antibiotics only if secondary infection
Avoid: aspirin, NSAIDs (worsen coagulopathy)
ddx: Non-venomous snakebite, Spider bite, Insect sting, Cellulitis, Allergic reaction, DIC (other causes)
labs:
CBC with platelets (serial q6h)
PT/INR, PTT, fibrinogen (coagulopathy)
BMP (rhabdomyolysis, renal function)
CPK
Type and screen
Wound cultures (if infection suspected)
Urine myoglobin
Serial circumference measurements of affected extremity (swelling progression)
notes:
US pit vipers: rattlesnake (most severe), copperhead (usually mild), cottonmouth (water moccasin)
Coral snake (elapid): neurotoxic, delayed onset, may need different antivenom
CroFab: Fab fragments, shorter half-life → recurrent coagulopathy in 50% (need maintenance dosing)
Anavip: F(ab')2, longer half-life, less recurrence
25% of pit viper bites are dry (no envenomation) - still observe
Indications for antivenom: progressive swelling, coagulopathy, systemic symptoms
Compartment syndrome: rare, check pressures before fasciotomy (antivenom often sufficient)
description: Poisoning from a venomous snake bite that can cause progressive tissue damage, blood clotting problems, and potentially life-threatening systemic effects, treated with specific antivenom.

name: Cyanide Poisoning
signs:
Rapid onset altered consciousness
Seizures
Bitter almond odor (not all can detect)
Cherry-red skin (classic but unreliable)
Tachycardia then bradycardia
Hypotension
Lactic acidosis despite normal PaO2
Respiratory failure
Cardiac arrest
treatment:
1. Remove from exposure, decontaminate
2. 100% oxygen
3. Hydroxocobalamin (Cyanokit) - preferred antidote
4. Sodium thiosulfate
5. Sodium nitrite (alternative, risk of methemoglobinemia)
6. Supportive care: intubation, vasopressors
7. Consider concurrent CO poisoning in fire victims
meds:
Hydroxocobalamin 5g IV over 15min (preferred antidote)
Sodium thiosulfate 12.5g IV over 10-20min
Sodium nitrite 300mg (10mL of 3%) IV over 5min (caution if CO co-exposure)
Amyl nitrite pearls inhaled (temporizing)
100% O2 via NRB or intubation
Sodium bicarbonate 1-2 mEq/kg IV (acidosis)
Epinephrine for cardiac arrest/hypotension
ddx: Carbon monoxide poisoning, Hydrogen sulfide, Methemoglobinemia, Sepsis, Metabolic acidosis (other), Seizure disorder, Cardiac arrest (other)
labs:
ABG/VBG: lactic acidosis with narrow A-V O2 difference (venous blood appears arterial)
Lactate markedly elevated
Cyanide level (send but don't wait for results - treat empirically)
CO-oximetry (concurrent CO in fire)
Methemoglobin level (if nitrites given)
BMP (anion gap acidosis)
ECG
notes:
Sources: house fires (combustion of synthetics), industrial (mining, electroplating), cyanogenic plants (cassava), nitroprusside toxicity
Mechanism: inhibits cytochrome c oxidase → blocks cellular respiration → histotoxic hypoxia
Cells can't use oxygen → high venous O2, lactic acidosis
Hydroxocobalamin: binds CN → cyanocobalamin (vitamin B12), safe, turns urine/skin red
Nitrite-thiosulfate kit: nitrite creates methemoglobin (binds CN), thiosulfate converts to thiocyanate (renally excreted)
Avoid nitrites if concurrent CO poisoning (worsens oxygen-carrying capacity)
Fire victims: treat empirically for both CN and CO poisoning
description: Poisoning from cyanide exposure that prevents cells from using oxygen, causing rapid collapse and death if not treated immediately with specific antidotes like hydroxocobalamin.

name: Methanol Poisoning
signs:
Inebriation initially (may mimic ethanol)
Visual disturbances (blurred vision, blindness)
Abdominal pain, nausea, vomiting
Headache
Altered mental status
Kussmaul breathing (acidosis)
Optic disc hyperemia on fundoscopy
Seizures (severe)
treatment:
1. Fomepizole (alcohol dehydrogenase inhibitor) - antidote
2. Correct metabolic acidosis with sodium bicarbonate
3. Hemodialysis for severe poisoning
4. Folinic acid (enhances formate metabolism)
5. Ethanol drip if fomepizole unavailable
6. Supportive care
meds:
Fomepizole 15mg/kg IV loading then 10mg/kg q12h
Sodium bicarbonate 1-2 mEq/kg IV (target pH >7.3)
Folinic acid (leucovorin) 50mg IV q6h
Ethanol drip 10% IV (target level 100-150mg/dL) if fomepizole unavailable
Thiamine 100mg IV (cofactor)
Hemodialysis
ddx: Ethanol intoxication, Ethylene glycol poisoning, DKA, Lactic acidosis, Isopropanol ingestion, Salicylate poisoning
labs:
ABG (severe anion gap metabolic acidosis)
Serum osmolality (elevated osmol gap early)
Methanol level (if available)
BMP (low bicarb, anion gap)
Osmol gap = measured - calculated osmolality (>10 = elevated)
Lactate
VBG
Urinalysis (no oxalate crystals = distinguishes from ethylene glycol)
Formate level (correlates with toxicity)
Fundoscopic exam
notes:
Methanol → formaldehyde → formic acid (toxic metabolite → retinal damage, metabolic acidosis)
Osmol gap elevated early (methanol), anion gap elevated late (formate accumulation)
Hemodialysis indications: visual symptoms, pH <7.25, renal failure, methanol >50mg/dL
Fomepizole blocks alcohol dehydrogenase → prevents conversion to toxic metabolites
Sources: windshield washer fluid, antifreeze, moonshine, industrial solvents
Visual toxicity: formate damages retinal ganglion cells and optic nerve
Ethylene glycol (antifreeze): similar treatment but causes calcium oxalate crystals in urine
description: Poisoning from ingesting methanol (wood alcohol) that produces toxic metabolites causing severe metabolic acidosis and blindness, requiring urgent treatment with fomepizole and potentially dialysis.

name: Hodgkin Lymphoma
signs:
Painless cervical/supraclavicular lymphadenopathy (most common)
B symptoms: fever, night sweats, weight loss >10% in 6 months
Pruritus
Alcohol-induced lymph node pain (rare but classic)
Mediastinal mass (cough, SVC syndrome)
Hepato/splenomegaly
Pel-Ebstein fever (cyclic)
treatment:
1. Early stage favorable: ABVD x2-4 cycles + involved-site radiation
2. Early stage unfavorable: ABVD x4-6 cycles +/- radiation
3. Advanced stage: ABVD x6 cycles or BV-AVD x6 cycles
4. PET-adapted therapy (escalate or de-escalate based on interim PET)
5. Relapsed/refractory: salvage chemo then autologous transplant
6. Brentuximab vedotin + checkpoint inhibitors for relapsed disease
meds:
ABVD: Adriamycin + Bleomycin + Vinblastine + Dacarbazine q2wk
BV-AVD: Brentuximab vedotin + AVD (omit bleomycin)
Brentuximab vedotin 1.8mg/kg IV q3wk
Pembrolizumab 200mg IV q3wk (relapsed)
Nivolumab 3mg/kg IV q2wk (relapsed)
ICE or DHAP (salvage regimens)
G-CSF (filgrastim) for neutropenia
ddx: Non-Hodgkin lymphoma, Reactive lymphadenopathy, Sarcoidosis, Infectious mononucleosis, Tuberculosis, Metastatic carcinoma, Castleman disease
labs:
Excisional lymph node biopsy (Reed-Sternberg cells, CD15+/CD30+)
PET-CT (staging, treatment response)
CT neck/chest/abdomen/pelvis
CBC, ESR (prognostic)
BMP, LFTs, albumin
LDH
HIV, hepatitis B/C
PFTs and echocardiogram (baseline before chemo)
Bone marrow biopsy (if PET positive in marrow)
Pregnancy test (reproductive-age women)
notes:
Bimodal age distribution: 15-35 and >55 years
Classic HL (95%): Reed-Sternberg cells in inflammatory background
Reed-Sternberg: large binucleated cells, owl-eye appearance, CD15+/CD30+
Subtypes: nodular sclerosis (most common, young adults), mixed cellularity, lymphocyte-rich, lymphocyte-depleted
One of the most curable cancers: >80% cure rate overall
Bleomycin pulmonary toxicity: monitor PFTs, avoid high FiO2
Fertility preservation: discuss sperm/egg banking before treatment
Secondary malignancies: breast cancer, lung cancer, MDS/AML (long-term risk from radiation/chemo)
description: A cancer of the lymphatic system characterized by the presence of distinctive Reed-Sternberg cells, typically presenting as painless lymph node swelling in young adults, with high cure rates.

name: Non-Hodgkin Lymphoma
signs:
Painless lymphadenopathy
B symptoms (fever, night sweats, weight loss)
Splenomegaly
Abdominal pain or fullness
Cytopenias (bone marrow involvement)
Skin lesions (cutaneous lymphoma)
CNS symptoms (primary CNS lymphoma)
Recurrent infections
treatment:
1. Indolent (follicular): watch and wait if asymptomatic, rituximab-based when needed
2. Aggressive (DLBCL): R-CHOP x6 cycles (curative intent)
3. Highly aggressive (Burkitt): intensive chemo (R-CODOX-M/IVAC or similar)
4. Mantle cell: rituximab + bendamustine or intensive chemo + auto-transplant
5. CAR T-cell therapy for relapsed aggressive NHL
6. Radiation for localized disease
meds:
R-CHOP: Rituximab + Cyclophosphamide + Doxorubicin + Vincristine + Prednisone q3wk
Rituximab 375mg/m2 IV
Bendamustine 90mg/m2 IV + rituximab (indolent)
CAR T-cell therapy (axicabtagene ciloleucel, lisocabtagene maraleucel)
Polatuzumab vedotin + R-CHP (DLBCL)
Lenalidomide + rituximab (indolent, maintenance)
Ibrutinib 560mg daily (mantle cell)
Tumor lysis prophylaxis: allopurinol, rasburicase, hydration
ddx: Hodgkin lymphoma, Reactive lymphadenopathy, Metastatic carcinoma, Sarcoidosis, CLL, Infections (TB, HIV, EBV), Castleman disease
labs:
Excisional lymph node biopsy (architecture + immunohistochemistry)
Flow cytometry
PET-CT (staging)
CT neck/chest/abdomen/pelvis
Bone marrow biopsy
CBC, LDH (prognostic), BMP, LFTs, uric acid
Hepatitis B (reactivation risk with rituximab)
HIV
FISH/cytogenetics (MYC, BCL2, BCL6 rearrangements)
LP if CNS lymphoma suspected
notes:
Most common hematologic malignancy
DLBCL: most common aggressive NHL (~30%), R-CHOP cures 60-70%
Follicular: most common indolent NHL, incurable but long survival (median >15 years)
Burkitt: highly aggressive, starry-sky pattern, MYC translocation t(8;14), cure rate >80% with intensive chemo
Double-hit lymphoma: MYC + BCL2 rearrangements, aggressive, treated with dose-adjusted chemo
Hep B reactivation: screen ALL patients before rituximab, give prophylaxis if HBsAg+ or anti-HBc+
Tumor lysis syndrome: high risk with Burkitt, high LDH, bulky disease - prophylax with rasburicase
CAR T: cytokine release syndrome and neurotoxicity are major complications
description: A diverse group of blood cancers arising from lymphocytes, ranging from slow-growing (indolent) types that may be watched to aggressive types requiring immediate chemotherapy.

name: Von Willebrand Disease
signs:
Easy bruising
Mucocutaneous bleeding (epistaxis, menorrhagia, gingival bleeding)
Excessive bleeding after surgery or dental procedures
Prolonged bleeding time
Family history of bleeding
GI bleeding (from angiodysplasia in type 2)
Postpartum hemorrhage
treatment:
1. Desmopressin (DDAVP) for type 1 and some type 2 (first-line)
2. VWF-containing factor concentrates (Humate-P) for major bleeding or type 3
3. Antifibrinolytics (tranexamic acid) for mucosal bleeding
4. Hormonal therapy for menorrhagia
5. Avoid aspirin and NSAIDs
6. Pre-procedural prophylaxis based on type and severity
meds:
Desmopressin 0.3mcg/kg IV/SC or 300mcg intranasal (type 1)
Humate-P (VWF:RCo/FVIII) dose based on VWF activity level
Tranexamic acid 1g PO TID or 10mg/kg IV
Aminocaproic acid 50-100mg/kg PO q6h
Oral contraceptives or IUD (menorrhagia)
Topical thrombin for minor wounds
ddx: Hemophilia A/B, Platelet function disorders, Acquired VWD, Liver disease, DIC, Anticoagulant use, ITP
labs:
VWF antigen level
VWF activity (ristocetin cofactor assay, VWF:GPIbM)
Factor VIII activity (reduced, VWF carries/stabilizes FVIII)
PTT (prolonged or normal)
Bleeding time or PFA-100 (prolonged)
CBC (thrombocytopenia in type 2B)
VWF multimer analysis (type classification)
Blood type (type O has lower VWF levels)
DDAVP challenge test
notes:
Most common inherited bleeding disorder (1% prevalence)
Type 1 (75-80%): quantitative deficiency, mild, responds to DDAVP
Type 2 (15-20%): qualitative defects (2A, 2B, 2M, 2N subtypes)
Type 3 (<5%): severe, absent VWF, factor VIII very low, resembles hemophilia A
Type 2B: DDAVP contraindicated (causes thrombocytopenia via platelet aggregation)
Blood type O: naturally lower VWF levels (may be misdiagnosed as type 1)
Pregnancy: VWF levels rise (may normalize), but risk of postpartum hemorrhage
Acquired VWD: associated with aortic stenosis, lymphoproliferative disorders, hypothyroidism
description: The most common inherited bleeding disorder, caused by deficient or defective von Willebrand factor, leading to impaired platelet adhesion and excessive mucocutaneous bleeding.

name: Polycythemia Vera
signs:
Plethoric facies (ruddy complexion)
Splenomegaly
Pruritus (especially after warm bath/shower - aquagenic pruritus)
Headache
Dizziness
Visual disturbances
Erythromelalgia (burning pain in hands/feet)
Thrombosis (stroke, DVT, Budd-Chiari)
Gout
treatment:
1. Phlebotomy to target Hct <45% (cornerstone)
2. Low-dose aspirin 81mg daily
3. Cytoreductive therapy if high-risk: hydroxyurea (first-line)
4. Ruxolitinib for hydroxyurea-intolerant/resistant
5. Manage cardiovascular risk factors
6. Monitor for transformation to myelofibrosis or AML
meds:
Phlebotomy (500mL) to maintain Hct <45%
Aspirin 81mg daily
Hydroxyurea 500-1500mg daily (high-risk: age >60 or prior thrombosis)
Ruxolitinib 10-20mg BID (JAK inhibitor, second-line)
Anagrelide 0.5mg BID (thrombocytosis)
Allopurinol 300mg daily (hyperuricemia)
Antihistamines or SSRIs for pruritus
ddx: Secondary polycythemia (hypoxia, EPO-secreting tumor), Chronic hypoxia (COPD, OSA), Dehydration (relative polycythemia), Other MPNs (ET, PMF), EPO-producing renal tumor
labs:
CBC: elevated Hgb (>16.5 men, >16 women), elevated Hct, often elevated WBC and platelets
JAK2 V617F mutation (positive in 95%)
JAK2 exon 12 (if V617F negative)
EPO level (low in PV, elevated in secondary)
Bone marrow biopsy (hypercellular, panmyelosis, pleomorphic megakaryocytes)
Iron studies (often depleted from phlebotomy)
Uric acid
LDH
Peripheral smear
notes:
JAK2 V617F positive in ~95% - essentially diagnostic
WHO criteria: all 3 major (Hgb >16.5/16 OR Hct >49%/48%, BM biopsy, JAK2) OR first 2 major + 1 minor (low EPO)
Hct <45% target: CYTO-PV trial showed reduced thrombosis
High-risk: age >60 OR prior thrombosis → cytoreduction
Low-risk: phlebotomy + aspirin only
Transformation: 10-20% evolve to myelofibrosis, 5-10% to AML over decades
Aquagenic pruritus: pathognomonic, treat with SSRIs, antihistamines, phototherapy
Budd-Chiari syndrome: hepatic vein thrombosis, think PV in young patients
description: A chronic blood cancer where the bone marrow produces too many red blood cells, increasing blood thickness and the risk of dangerous blood clots, treated primarily with blood removal (phlebotomy).

name: Chronic Venous Insufficiency
signs:
Lower extremity edema (worse at end of day, improves with elevation)
Varicose veins
Skin discoloration (hemosiderin staining - brown)
Venous stasis dermatitis (eczematous changes)
Lipodermatosclerosis (indurated, inverted champagne bottle shape)
Venous ulcers (medial malleolus)
Pain/heaviness in legs with prolonged standing
treatment:
1. Compression therapy (30-40mmHg stockings) - cornerstone
2. Leg elevation above heart level
3. Exercise (calf muscle pump activation)
4. Wound care for venous ulcers (moist dressing, compression)
5. Endovenous ablation (laser or radiofrequency) for saphenous reflux
6. Sclerotherapy for varicose veins
7. Skin care to prevent breakdown
meds:
Compression stockings 30-40mmHg (knee-high or thigh-high)
Multilayer compression bandaging (acute ulcer management)
Topical emollients (skin hydration)
Topical corticosteroids (stasis dermatitis, short-term)
Pentoxifylline 400mg TID (adjunct for venous ulcers)
Aspirin 300mg daily (may improve ulcer healing)
Diuretics NOT routinely recommended
ddx: Lymphedema, DVT, PAD (arterial ulcers), CHF, Cellulitis, Lipoedema, Contact dermatitis, Nephrotic syndrome
labs:
Duplex ultrasound (venous reflux, >0.5sec reflux = significant)
ABI (rule out PAD before compression - ABI must be >0.8)
Wound culture if ulcer appears infected
CBC, BMP, albumin
HbA1c (diabetes impairs healing)
Venous plethysmography (if ultrasound inconclusive)
notes:
CEAP classification: C0-C6 based on clinical severity
C6 = active venous ulcer (medial malleolus is classic location)
ABI MUST be checked before applying compression (PAD + compression = disastrous)
Venous ulcers: shallow, irregular borders, medial malleolus, moist base
Arterial ulcers: deep, punched out, lateral malleolus/toes, painful, dry base
Compression reduces ulcer recurrence from 70% to 20%
Superficial venous ablation improves ulcer healing and reduces recurrence
Lifelong compression is recommended to prevent recurrence
description: A condition where leg veins cannot efficiently return blood to the heart, causing swelling, skin changes, and potentially painful ulcers near the ankles, managed primarily with compression therapy.

name: Thoracic Aortic Aneurysm
signs:
Often asymptomatic (found incidentally on imaging)
Chest or back pain (expansion/rupture)
Hoarseness (recurrent laryngeal nerve compression)
Dysphagia (esophageal compression)
SVC syndrome
Aortic regurgitation murmur
Unequal pulses
Widened mediastinum on CXR
treatment:
1. Blood pressure control (beta-blocker first-line)
2. Surveillance imaging at regular intervals
3. Surgery when size threshold reached or rapid growth
4. Open surgical repair or endovascular (TEVAR)
5. Genetic testing if Marfan or familial TAA
6. Screen first-degree relatives of familial cases
7. Avoid heavy lifting (isometric exercise)
meds:
Losartan 50-100mg daily (Marfan syndrome, preferred over BB)
Atenolol 25-100mg daily (beta-blocker)
Amlodipine 5-10mg daily (add-on BP control)
Atorvastatin 40-80mg daily
Target SBP <130mmHg
Avoid fluoroquinolones (associated with aortic dissection/aneurysm)
ddx: Aortic dissection, Mediastinal mass, Lung cancer, Thymoma, Pericardial effusion, Esophageal disorders
labs:
CT angiography (gold standard for size and morphology)
TTE/TEE (aortic root, ascending aorta, valve)
MRA (surveillance, no radiation)
CXR (widened mediastinum, tracheal deviation)
Genetic testing: FBN1 (Marfan), TGFBR1/2 (Loeys-Dietz), ACTA2, MYH11
Lipid panel, HbA1c
ECG (LVH from aortic regurgitation)
notes:
Surgical thresholds: ascending >5.5cm (5.0cm if Marfan/bicuspid, 4.5cm if Loeys-Dietz), descending >6.0-6.5cm
Rapid growth >0.5cm/year or >1cm total: consider surgery regardless of size
Marfan syndrome: FBN1 mutation, aortic root dilation, lens subluxation, tall habitus
Bicuspid aortic valve: associated ascending aortic aneurysm (lower surgical threshold 5.0cm)
TEVAR: endovascular repair for descending thoracic aneurysms (lower morbidity than open)
Acute rupture: mortality >90% if prehospital, 50% if reaches OR
Fluoroquinolone warning: FDA black box for aortic dissection/aneurysm risk
description: An abnormal widening of the thoracic aorta that usually causes no symptoms but can be life-threatening if it ruptures or dissects, monitored with imaging and repaired surgically when large enough.

name: Arteriovenous Malformation
signs:
Often asymptomatic until hemorrhage
Intracranial hemorrhage (50% present with bleeding)
Seizures
Headache
Focal neurological deficits
Pulsatile mass (peripheral AVM)
Bruit over lesion
Heart failure (large AVMs with high-flow shunting)
treatment:
1. Multidisciplinary evaluation (neurosurgery, interventional neuroradiology)
2. Surgical resection (definitive for accessible AVMs)
3. Stereotactic radiosurgery (Gamma Knife) for deep/small AVMs
4. Endovascular embolization (pre-surgical or standalone)
5. Observation for unruptured AVMs in some patients (ARUBA trial)
6. Manage seizures if present
7. Hemorrhage: standard ICH management, consider AVM-directed treatment
meds:
Levetiracetam 500-1500mg BID (seizures)
Labetalol IV (BP control during hemorrhage)
Mannitol 1g/kg IV (elevated ICP)
Dexamethasone (peritumoral edema from radiosurgery)
Analgesics for headache
Anticoagulants generally AVOIDED
ddx: Brain tumor, Cavernous malformation, Dural AV fistula, Aneurysm, Arteriovenous fistula, Hemorrhagic stroke (other causes), Developmental venous anomaly
labs:
CT head (acute hemorrhage)
CT angiography (identify AVM)
Brain MRI with MRA (characterize nidus, feeding arteries, draining veins)
Conventional cerebral angiography (gold standard, shows angioarchitecture)
EEG if seizures
Spetzler-Martin grading (size, eloquence, venous drainage → surgical risk)
notes:
Spetzler-Martin grade: I-II low risk (surgery favored), III (individualized), IV-V high risk (observation or radiosurgery)
ARUBA trial: unruptured AVM observation had better outcomes than intervention (controversial, criticized)
Ruptured AVMs: higher rebleed risk (2-4%/year), more likely to benefit from treatment
Radiosurgery: takes 2-3 years for obliteration, hemorrhage risk persists until obliterated
Hereditary hemorrhagic telangiectasia (HHT/Osler-Weber-Rendu): multiple AVMs in lungs, brain, liver, GI
Annual hemorrhage risk: 2-4% for unruptured, higher for ruptured
Pediatric AVMs: more likely to present with hemorrhage, consider treatment
description: An abnormal tangle of blood vessels connecting arteries directly to veins without normal capillaries in between, most dangerous when occurring in the brain where rupture can cause hemorrhagic stroke.
